summary: Gruber responds to a Forbes article listing seven iPhone disappointments by reframing each criticism as a deliberate design trade-off rather than a failure. He argues that most complaints — cost, no Flash, sealed batteries, camera quality, no video recording — reflect reasonable engineering decisions where Apple chose one benefit over another. The few genuine omissions like copy-and-paste and MMS are attributed to Apple simply not having gotten to them yet. He draws a parallel to the iPod's gradual evolution, noting that the iPhone platform is actually moving faster than the iPod did, adding 3G, GPS, and a full SDK in just one year. His core message is that patience is warranted given Apple's track record of iterative improvement.
key_points: Most iPhone criticisms are actually trade-offs where Apple made defensible choices favoring thinness, battery life, and simplicity over feature checklists
The iPhone without Flash only compares badly against an imaginary iPhone with Flash, not any actual competing phone
Copy-and-paste is the one genuine head-scratching omission that Gruber can't rationalize as a trade-off
The iPhone's evolution is tracking faster than the iPod's — the App Store arrives in year one versus the iTunes Store's two-year wait
Apple's strategy is deliberate incremental improvement: one new thing at a time, as proven by the iPod's history
money_quotes: A big part of design is managing trade-offs.
The iPhone without Flash only compares badly against an imaginary iPhone with Flash, not any actual existing competing phone.
You know a list of iPhone 'disappointments' is getting thin when the swappable battery thing comes up as #3.
But people in hell want ice water.
To ask for better image quality or video support without acknowledging that it would make the iPhone more expensive (and probably thicker and heavier) is to ignore the inherent trade-offs.
There are times when I want to copy and paste something on my iPhone — usually a URL — so badly that it almost literally hurts my fingers that I can't.
You can't keep adding features without pushing the release date for new software forward.
key_insight: Most criticisms of the iPhone reflect inherent engineering trade-offs that Apple has managed well, and the platform's rapid evolution compared to the iPod suggests patience will be rewarded.
